content:
# Luke Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 102-104 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_012_page_001.jpg, LUK_012_page_002.jpg, LUK_012_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** In which [time], the myriads of the crowd having been gathered together, so as to trample one another, he began to say to his disciples first, "Guard yourselves from the leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy.

**2.** But nothing has been covered-over which shall not be uncovered, and hidden which shall not be made known.

**3.** Therefore, whatever [things] you said in the darkness shall be heard in the light, and what you spoke to the ear in the inner-rooms shall be proclaimed upon the housetops.

**4.** And I say to you, my friends, do not be afraid of those killing the body, and after these [things] not having anything more to do.

**5.** But I will show you whom you should fear: fear the [one] having authority, after the killing, to cast into Gehenna. Yes, I say to you, fear this [one].

**6.** Are not five sparrows sold for two assaria? And not one of them is forgotten before God.

**7.** But even the hairs of your head have all been numbered. Do not fear; you are of-more-value than many sparrows.

**8.** And I say to you, everyone who shall confess in me before men, the Son of Man also shall confess in him before the angels of God.

**9.** But the [one] having denied me before men shall be denied before the angels of God.

**10.** And everyone who shall speak a word against the Son of Man, it shall be forgiven him; but to the [one] having blasphemed against the Holy Spirit, it shall not be forgiven.

**11.** And when they bring you before the synagogues and the rulers and the authorities, do not be anxious how or what you should speak-in-defense, or what you should say;

**12.** for the Holy Spirit shall teach you in that very hour what [things] it is necessary to say."

**13.** And a certain one from the crowd said to him, "Teacher, tell my brother to divide the inheritance with me."

**14.** But he said to him, "Man, who appointed me [a] judge or divider over you?"

**15.** And he said to them, "Watch and guard yourselves from all covetousness, because not in the abounding of [things] to someone is his life out of his possessions."

**16.** And he spoke a parable to them, saying, "A certain rich man's land bore-well.

**17.** And he was reasoning within himself, saying, 'What shall I do, because I do not have where I shall gather together my fruits?'

**18.** And he said, 'This I will do: I will tear down my barns and build greater [ones], and I will gather together there all my grain and my goods.

**19.** And I will say to my soul, "Soul, you have many goods laid up for many years; rest, eat, drink, be merry."'

**20.** But God said to him, 'Senseless [one], this night they demand your soul from you; and the [things] which you prepared, to whom shall they be?'

**21.** So [is] the [one] treasuring-up for himself, and not being rich toward God."

**22.** And he said to his disciples, "On account of this I say to you, do not be anxious [for] the soul, what you should eat, nor [for] the body, what you should put on.

**23.** For the soul is more than the food, and the body [more] than the clothing.

**24.** Consider the ravens, that they do not sow nor reap, to which there is no storehouse nor barn, and God feeds them. How much more you are of-more-value than the birds!

**25.** And who of you, being anxious, is able to add upon his stature a [single] cubit?

**26.** If therefore you are not able [to do] the least, why are you anxious about the rest?

**27.** Consider the lilies, how they grow: they do not labor nor spin; but I say to you, not even Solomon in all his glory clothed himself like one of these.

**28.** But if the grass in [the] field, being [here] today and tomorrow being cast into [an] oven, God so clothes, how much more you, [ones] of-little-faith!

**29.** And you, do not seek what you shall eat and what you shall drink, and do not be anxious.

**30.** For all these [things] the nations of the world seek after; but your Father knows that you have need of these [things].

**31.** Nevertheless, seek his kingdom, and these [things] shall be added to you.

**32.** Do not fear, the little flock, because your Father was well-pleased to give you the kingdom.

**33.** Sell your possessions and give alms. Make for yourselves purses not growing-old, a treasure unfailing in the heavens, where a thief does not draw near, nor a moth destroys.

**34.** For where your treasure is, there also your heart will be.

**35.** Let your loins be girded and the lamps burning,

**36.** and you [be] like men waiting-for their own lord, when he should return from the wedding-feasts, so that, [when] he comes and knocks, immediately they may open to him.

**37.** Blessed [are] those slaves whom the lord, having come, shall find watching. Truly I say to you that he shall gird himself and shall cause them to recline, and having come near, he shall serve them.

**38.** And if he should come in the second watch, and in the third watch should come, and find [them] so, blessed are those [slaves].

**39.** But know this, that if the master-of-the-house had known what hour the thief comes, he would not have allowed his house to be dug-through.

**40.** And you, become ready, because at [the] hour you do not think, the Son of Man comes."

**41.** And Peter said, "Lord, do you speak this parable to us, or also to all?"

**42.** And the Lord said, "Who then is the faithful steward, the wise [one], whom the lord shall set over his household-servants, to give the portion-of-grain in [due] season?

**43.** Blessed [is] that slave whom his lord, having come, shall find doing so.

**44.** Truly I say to you that he shall set him over all his possessions.

**45.** But if that slave should say in his heart, 'My lord delays to come,' and should begin to beat the male-servants and the female-servants, and to eat and drink and be drunk,

**46.** the lord of that slave shall come on a day in which he does not expect, and in an hour which he does not know, and shall cut-him-asunder, and shall place his portion with the unfaithful.

**47.** And that slave, the [one] having known the will of his lord, and not having prepared or done according to his will, shall be beaten [with] many [blows].

**48.** But the [one] not having known, but having done [things] worthy of blows, shall be beaten [with] few. And to everyone to whom much was given, much shall be sought from him; and to whom they entrusted much, more abundantly they shall ask of him.

**49.** I came to cast fire upon the earth, and what do I wish if it has already been kindled?

**50.** But I have a baptism to be baptized [with], and how am I pressed-together until it should be completed!

**51.** Do you think that I came to give peace on the earth? No, I say to you, but rather division.

**52.** For from now [on] there shall be five in one house having been divided, three against two, and two against three;

**53.** [they shall be divided], father against son, and son against father; mother against daughter, and daughter against the mother; mother-in-law against her daughter-in-law, and daughter-in-law against the mother-in-law."

**54.** And he was saying also to the crowds, "When you see a cloud rising from [the] west, immediately you say, 'A rainstorm is coming,' and so it happens.

**55.** And when [you see] a south [wind] blowing, you say, 'There will be burning-heat,' and it happens.

**56.** Hypocrites! The face of the earth and of the heaven you know [how] to discern, but this season, how do you not know [how] to discern?

**57.** And why also from yourselves do you not judge what [is] right?

**58.** For as you are going with your adversary before [a] ruler, on the way give effort to be released from him, lest he drag you to the judge, and the judge hand you over to the officer, and the officer cast you into prison.

**59.** I say to you, you shall not come out from there until you have paid the very last lepton."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **12:1 — "the myriads of the crowd" (των μυριαδων του οχλου):** Sinaiticus reads των μυριαδων του οχλου, literally "the myriads (ten-thousands) of the crowd." This is hyperbolic language indicating an enormous gathering — so vast that people trample one another. The word μυριας can mean literally "ten thousand" or figuratively "an innumerable multitude." Despite this massive audience, Jesus addresses his disciples "first" (πρωτον), establishing a pedagogical priority: the inner circle receives teaching before the wider crowd.

2. **12:1 — "leaven of the Pharisees, which is hypocrisy" (της ζυμης...ητις εστιν υποκρισις):** Sinaiticus reads ητις εστιν υποκρισις, explicitly identifying the leaven as hypocrisy. The relative pronoun ητις ("which is of such a nature that") implies an essential identification, not a mere comparison. Leaven (ζυμη) in Jewish and early Christian usage typically symbolizes a pervasive corrupting influence (cf. 1 Corinthians 5:6-8; Galatians 5:9). In Mark 8:15, the leaven is left unidentified; Luke's Jesus provides the interpretation. The word υποκρισις originally denoted an actor's performance on stage — the Pharisees' piety is theatrical.

3. **12:5 — "having authority...to cast into Gehenna" (εξουσιαν...εμβαλειν εις την γεενναν):** Sinaiticus reads εξουσιαν εχοντα μετα το αποκτειναι εμβαλειν εις την γεενναν. The one to be feared has authority (εξουσια) both to kill and afterward to cast into Gehenna. This can only be God — not Satan, who lacks this dual authority. Γεεννα (Gehenna) derives from Hebrew ge-hinnom, the Valley of Hinnom south of Jerusalem, where child sacrifice was once practiced (2 Kings 23:10; Jeremiah 7:31) and which became the symbolic place of eschatological punishment. This is the sole Lukan occurrence of the term.

4. **12:6 — "two assaria" (ασσαριων δυο):** Sinaiticus reads ασσαριων δυο. An assarion was a small Roman copper coin worth one-sixteenth of a denarius. Matthew 10:29 states two sparrows for one assarion; Luke states five for two assaria — an even cheaper rate (a bulk discount, as it were), underscoring the utter insignificance of sparrows in the marketplace. Yet not one is "forgotten before God" (επιλελησμενον ενωπιον του θεου). The perfect participle implies God's memory is permanent and active.

5. **12:8 — "confess in me" (ομολογηση εν εμοι):** Sinaiticus reads ομολογηση εν εμοι, literally "shall confess in me." The preposition εν with ομολογεω is a Semitism reflecting the Hebrew/Aramaic idiom of "acknowledging in" someone — publicly declaring allegiance. The Son of Man reciprocates this confession "before the angels of God" (εμπροσθεν των αγγελων του θεου), a circumlocution for the divine court. Luke consistently uses "angels of God" where Matthew uses "my Father in the heavens" (Matthew 10:32), reflecting Luke's distinct heavenly-court imagery.

6. **12:10 — The Unforgivable Sin:** Sinaiticus reads τω δε εις το αγιον πνευμα βλασφημησαντι ουκ αφεθησεται, "but to the [one] having blasphemed against the Holy Spirit, it shall not be forgiven." The placement differs significantly from Mark 3:28-30 and Matthew 12:31-32, where this saying occurs in the context of the Beelzebul controversy. Luke places it in the context of persecution and public testimony (12:8-12), suggesting that blasphemy against the Spirit involves the deliberate, sustained rejection of the Spirit's testimony about Jesus — not a single utterance but a settled disposition against the Spirit's witness.

7. **12:11 — "how or what you should speak-in-defense" (πως η τι απολογησησθε):** Sinaiticus reads πως η τι απολογησησθε η τι ειπητε. The verb απολογεομαι is a legal term — "to make a formal defense" in a judicial setting. The three-fold concern (how, what defense, what to say) covers both the manner and content of testimony. The promise of the Holy Spirit's instruction "in that very hour" (εν αυτη τη ωρα) presupposes an immediacy of divine provision — no advance preparation is needed because the Spirit will teach (διδαξει) in real time.

8. **12:14 — "judge or divider" (κριτην η μεριστην):** Sinaiticus reads κριτην η μεριστην. The word μεριστης ("divider/arbitrator") occurs only here in the New Testament. Jesus refuses the role of inheritance arbitrator — a common function for rabbis in Jewish society. The refusal is significant: Jesus' mission concerns the kingdom of God, not property disputes. The echo of Moses' experience is deliberate (cf. Exodus 2:14, "Who made you a ruler and judge over us?"), but with inverted meaning.

9. **12:15 — "all covetousness" (πασης πλεονεξιας):** Sinaiticus reads πασης πλεονεξιας, "every [form of] covetousness." The word πλεονεξια literally means "a desire to have more" — from πλεον ("more") + εχω ("to have"). Paul lists it alongside idolatry (Colossians 3:5), and the Decalogue's tenth commandment prohibits it. The saying that follows — "not in the abounding...is his life out of his possessions" — states the principle the parable will illustrate: life (ζωη) cannot be secured by accumulation.

10. **12:16 — "bore-well" (ευφορησεν):** Sinaiticus reads ευφορησεν, from ευφορεω ("to bear well/produce abundantly"). This is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament. The compound εὐ + φορεω emphasizes the exceptional productivity of the land. Critically, the rich man takes no credit for the harvest — the land itself produces — yet he immediately claims personal ownership: "my fruits," "my barns," "my grain," "my goods," "my soul." The seven-fold repetition of "my" (μου) exposes the self-absorption.

11. **12:19 — "Soul...rest, eat, drink, be merry" (ψυχη...αναπαυου, φαγε, πιε, ευφραινου):** Sinaiticus reads this four-fold imperative sequence. The address to one's own soul (ψυχη) echoes Ecclesiastes 2:24 and 8:15, but with devastating irony: the fool's self-congratulation is immediately interrupted by God's verdict. The verb ευφραινου ("be merry/celebrate") will recur in the Prodigal Son parable (15:23, 24, 29, 32) with redemptive meaning — here it represents the hollow merriment of self-indulgence.

12. **12:20 — "Senseless one" (αφρων):** Sinaiticus reads αφρων, the same word Jesus used of the Pharisees in 11:40. In the Septuagint, the αφρων is the moral fool of Wisdom literature — one who lives as though God does not exist (Psalm 13:1 LXX). The passive "they demand" (απαιτουσιν) may refer to angels of death — the subject is deliberately impersonal, emphasizing the fool's utter helplessness. The question "to whom shall they be?" (τινι εσται) exposes the absurdity of accumulation without regard for mortality.

13. **12:21 — "rich toward God" (εις θεον πλουτων):** Sinaiticus reads εις θεον πλουτων, "being rich toward/into God." The preposition εις suggests direction and purpose — wealth directed toward God through generosity, almsgiving, and faithful stewardship. This phrase is unique to Luke and provides the moral framework for the entire chapter: true wealth is relational and vertical, not material and horizontal.

14. **12:25 — "stature" or "lifespan" (ηλικιαν...πηχυν):** Sinaiticus reads επι την ηλικιαν αυτου προσθειναι πηχυν. The word ηλικια can mean either "stature" (physical height) or "span of life" (age/duration). A πηχυς ("cubit") is approximately eighteen inches — a trivial addition to height but a significant extension of life. In context, since anxiety about food and clothing relates to sustaining life, "lifespan" is more likely the intended sense: adding even a small measure to one's life is beyond human power.

15. **12:27 — "they do not labor nor spin" (ου κοπια ουδε νηθει):** Sinaiticus reads ου κοπια ουδε νηθει, with singular verbs. The verb κοπιαω ("to labor/toil") typically describes heavy agricultural work, while νηθω ("to spin") describes women's textile work. Together they encompass the full range of productive labor. The lilies' glory surpasses Solomon's — the wealthiest king of Israel who was proverbial for magnificence (1 Kings 10:4-7). Nature's beauty, freely given by God, exceeds the greatest human achievement.

16. **12:28 — "ones of-little-faith" (ολιγοπιστοι):** Sinaiticus reads ολιγοπιστοι, a compound adjective meaning "of little faith." This word appears four times in Matthew but only here in Luke. It is a gentle rebuke — not unbelief but insufficient trust. The a fortiori argument is powerful: if God lavishes beauty on grass destined for the oven (dried grass was used as fuel), how much more will he provide for those made in his image?

17. **12:29 — "do not be anxious" (μη μετεωριζεσθε):** Sinaiticus reads μη μετεωριζεσθε, from μετεωριζω, literally "do not be raised up/suspended in mid-air." This rare verb (a New Testament hapax) metaphorically describes the mind tossed about in anxious suspense — wavering between hope and fear, never at rest. The image is of being lifted up like a ship on a stormy sea, without an anchor. It is a stronger and more vivid term than the simple μεριμναω ("be anxious") used earlier in the passage.

18. **12:32 — "the little flock" (το μικρον ποιμνιον):** Sinaiticus reads το μικρον ποιμνιον, "the little flock." The diminutive ποιμνιον intensifies the sense of smallness and vulnerability. This is the only occurrence of ποιμνιον in the Gospels. The image of God's people as a flock is deeply rooted in the Hebrew Scriptures (Psalm 23; Isaiah 40:11; Ezekiel 34). The contrast between the flock's smallness and the Father's gift of the kingdom is the point: divine generosity is not proportional to human merit or might.

19. **12:33 — "purses not growing-old" (βαλλαντια μη παλαιουμενα):** Sinaiticus reads βαλλαντια μη παλαιουμενα, "money-bags not growing old/wearing out." The contrast is between earthly purses that deteriorate and heavenly treasure that is ανεκλειπτον ("unfailing/inexhaustible"). The language of selling possessions and giving alms (ελεημοσυνην) is distinctively Lukan and radical — not merely tithing but liquidating assets for the sake of the poor. This anticipates the practice of the Jerusalem church (Acts 2:45, 4:34-37).

20. **12:35 — "let your loins be girded" (εστωσαν υμων αι οσφυες περιεζωσμεναι):** Sinaiticus reads this periphrastic perfect passive imperative. The girded loins recall the Passover meal eaten in readiness for departure (Exodus 12:11). Long robes were tucked into the belt for active work or travel. Combined with "the lamps burning" (οι λυχνοι καιομενοι), the image is of servants in a state of perpetual readiness — prepared for the master's return at any moment.

21. **12:37 — "he shall gird himself and shall cause them to recline" (περιζωσεται...ανακλινει):** Sinaiticus reads this astonishing reversal: the master (κυριος) girds himself like a servant and serves (διακονησει) his slaves. This overturns every social expectation of the ancient world. The verb ανακλινω ("to cause to recline at table") places the slaves in the honored position of banquet guests. This anticipates Jesus' own actions at the Last Supper (22:27: "I am among you as the one serving") and the foot-washing in John 13. No earthly master would do this — the parable reveals God's character.

22. **12:38 — "second watch...third watch" (δευτερα φυλακη...τριτη φυλακη):** Sinaiticus reads δευτερα and τριτη φυλακη. The Jewish night was divided into three watches (evening, midnight, cockcrow), while the Roman system used four. Luke's reference to the second and third watches (roughly 10 PM to 2 AM and 2 AM to 6 AM) indicates the latest, most unlikely hours — when alertness is most difficult. The blessing is pronounced specifically on those who remain watchful when others sleep.

23. **12:39 — "dug-through" (διορυχθηναι):** Sinaiticus reads διορυχθηναι, from διορυσσω ("to dig through"). Ancient Palestinian houses had mud-brick walls that could literally be excavated by thieves (cf. Job 24:16; Matthew 6:19). The householder metaphor shifts the emphasis: whereas the servants were praised for vigilance, here the point is the unpredictability of the moment. The Son of Man's coming is likened to a thief's — not in moral character but in unexpectedness.

24. **12:42 — "the faithful steward, the wise" (ο πιστος οικονομος ο φρονιμος):** Sinaiticus reads ο πιστος οικονομος ο φρονιμος. The οικονομος ("household-steward/manager") was a position of significant trust in Greco-Roman households — often a slave given authority over the entire domestic economy. The two adjectives πιστος ("faithful/trustworthy") and φρονιμος ("wise/prudent") define the steward's essential qualities. The "portion-of-grain" (σιτομετριον, a New Testament hapax) was the measured food ration distributed to household servants — faithful stewardship involves providing for others' needs.

25. **12:46 — "shall cut-him-asunder" (διχοτομησει):** Sinaiticus reads διχοτομησει, from διχοτομεω ("to cut in two/cut asunder"). This brutal verb literally describes bisection — a punishment attested in the ancient Near East (cf. 2 Samuel 12:31; Hebrews 11:37; Susanna 55, 59 in the LXX). Whether literal or metaphorical (meaning "to punish with utmost severity"), the image is deliberately horrifying. The unfaithful slave's "portion" (μερος) is placed "with the unfaithful" (μετα των απιστων) — Luke's word απιστος ("unfaithful/unbelieving") replaces Matthew's "hypocrites" (24:51), emphasizing the betrayal of trust.

26. **12:47-48 — Graduated Punishment:** Sinaiticus preserves this uniquely Lukan teaching on proportional accountability. The slave who knew (γνους) his master's will and did not act receives many blows (πολλας); the one who did not know (μη γνους) but acted wrongly receives few (ολιγας). The principle is stated explicitly: "to whom much was given, much shall be sought" (παντι δε ω εδοθη πολυ πολυ ζητηθησεται). The verb παρεθεντο ("they entrusted") adds a second dimension: those given greater responsibility face stricter accounting. This graded judgment reflects a principle of proportional justice absent from the Matthean parallel.

27. **12:49 — "fire upon the earth" (πυρ...επι την γην):** Sinaiticus reads πυρ ηλθον βαλειν επι την γην, "fire I came to cast upon the earth." This saying is unique to Luke and intensely debated. Fire in the biblical tradition is multivalent: (a) purifying judgment (Malachi 3:2-3), (b) the Holy Spirit's descent at Pentecost (Acts 2:3), (c) division and conflict (the immediate context of vv. 51-53). The phrase τι θελω ει ηδη ανηφθη ("what do I wish if it has already been kindled?") is grammatically difficult; it may express eager desire ("how I wish it were already kindled!") or existential tension about the conflagration Jesus knows he will ignite.

28. **12:50 — "baptism" (βαπτισμα):** Sinaiticus reads βαπτισμα δε εχω βαπτισθηναι, "but a baptism I have to be baptized [with]." The metaphorical use of βαπτισμα for suffering and death is well attested (cf. Mark 10:38-39). The verb συνεχομαι ("I am pressed-together/constrained") conveys intense psychological compression — Jesus is hemmed in, unable to find relief until this baptism of suffering is accomplished (τελεσθη). The passive voice of τελεσθη ("be completed/accomplished") echoes the divine necessity (δει) of the passion.

29. **12:51 — "division" (διαμερισμον):** Sinaiticus reads αλλ η διαμερισμον, "but rather division." The noun διαμερισμος (a New Testament hapax) means "a thorough dividing/distribution." Matthew 10:34 reads "a sword" (μαχαιραν) instead. Luke's term is less militaristic but equally radical: the division cuts through the most intimate bonds of family. The household catalogue that follows (vv. 52-53) echoes Micah 7:6, a prophetic text about the dissolution of social bonds in the eschatological period.

30. **12:53 — Family Division:** Sinaiticus reads the list of household divisions with five pairs: father/son, son/father, mother/daughter, daughter/mother, mother-in-law/daughter-in-law, daughter-in-law/mother-in-law. The division is bidirectional — it is not simply the young against the old or the old against the young, but mutual rupture. This allusion to Micah 7:6 was understood in Judaism as describing the birth-pangs of the Messiah (the tribulation preceding the messianic age). Jesus' coming precipitates this eschatological crisis within the family unit.

31. **12:54-56 — Reading the Signs:** Sinaiticus preserves this weather parable unique to Luke in this form. A cloud from the west (απο δυσμων) — coming from the Mediterranean Sea — signals rain; a south wind (νοτον) from the Negev desert signals scorching heat. These are elementary observations of Palestinian weather patterns. The crowd's ability to interpret nature but not "this season" (τον καιρον τουτον) is the central irony. The word καιρος denotes not chronological time (χρονος) but a decisive, opportune moment — the time of God's visitation in Jesus' ministry.

32. **12:56 — "Hypocrites" (υποκριται):** Sinaiticus reads υποκριται, forming an inclusio with verse 1 where the leaven of the Pharisees was identified as υποκρισις ("hypocrisy"). The chapter thus opens and closes with the theme of hypocrisy — the inability or refusal to perceive spiritual reality despite possessing the necessary faculties. The crowds can read the sky but not the times; the Pharisees can perform religion but not live it.

33. **12:58 — "give effort to be released" (δος εργασιαν απηλλαχθαι):** Sinaiticus reads δος εργασιαν απηλλαχθαι, literally "give work/effort to have been released." The legal metaphor envisions a debtor being dragged before a magistrate. The urgency of settlement "on the way" (εν τη οδω) — before reaching the court — applies the weather-reading analogy to personal responsibility: just as one reads weather signs and acts accordingly, one should read the signs of the times and urgently seek reconciliation with God before judgment comes.

34. **12:59 — "the very last lepton" (τον εσχατον λεπτον):** Sinaiticus reads τον εσχατον λεπτον, "the last lepton." The λεπτον was the smallest Jewish coin, worth half a Roman quadrans (cf. Mark 12:42) — approximately one-128th of a denarius. The phrase "the very last" (εσχατον) emphasizes the thoroughness of the judgment: every last debt must be paid. The passage functions as an eschatological warning wrapped in a legal parable — the time for reconciliation is now, before the final accounting from which there is no escape.
